persons.arguments_against: Gordon is the commonest surname in this corpus (117 staff-list officials, 47 biographies), so the case for a mistake is a second Arthur Hamilton Gordon. It fails on every dimension. Fiji stint: the biography reads 'governor of the new colony of Fiji, Jan., 1876' and the staff list shows Governor and Commander-in-Chief 1877-1880; the courtesy style matches ('The Hon. Sir Arthur Hamilton Gordon' against the bio's 'HON. SIR ARTHUR HAMILTON'); the honours upgrade in the staff list from K.C.M.G. (1877, 1878) to G.C.M.G. (1879 onward) exactly tracks the bio's K.C.M.G. (1871) and G.C.M.G. (1878); and the stint's final 1883 row, 'Honorary Commissioner for the Western Pacific', echoes the bio's 'high commissioner for the Western Pacific, 1877'. Ceylon stint: the bio ends 'governor of Ceylon, 1883' and the staff list shows Sir Arthur Hamilton Gordon, G.C.M.G., as Governor from 1886 through 1889-90 — the same unique office, same full double forename, same G.C.M.G. Both governorships are sole-occupant posts; a namesake would have to hold the identical full name, identical honours with identical award sequence, and the same two governorships. No live counter-hypothesis remains.

evidence_cited.fact: governor of the new colony of Fiji, Jan., 1876
high commissioner for the Western Pacific, 1877

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second Sir Arthur Hamilton Gordon, G.C.M.G., in colonial service in the 1870s-1880s, or gazette records naming different governors of Fiji 1877-1880 or Ceylon 1886-1890.

notes: The G.C.M.G. switch between the 1878 and 1879 Fiji staff lists is a precise, independent corroboration of the bio's 1878 award. The gap between the bio's Ceylon appointment (1883) and the stint's first record (1886) reflects edition coverage, not a tenure conflict — the bio appears in the 1883 edition already naming him governor of Ceylon.
